epic: Foundation: Repository structure, types, and core systems

issues.title: [Foundation][P0] Restructure repository for app code, assets, and docs
body_lines: The top level currently mixes source, configs, docs, shell scripts, Docker, Firebase config, and many sprite/image files (86 loose image assets at repo root).
That makes it harder for any single agent to reason about ownership, build inputs, and rendering assets.
## Scope
- Move loose image assets (86 files: penis*.png, chest*.png, etc.) into a clear asset hierarchy.
- Define where authoritative game assets live: `public/`, `assets/`, or `src/assets/`.
- Decide whether `project/` and `revenue-sidecar/` are production code, experiments, or separate deployables.
- Add/update README sections for architecture and folder ownership.
- Remove or document duplicate/legacy folders (e.g., `Hikari_Female_Sideview_And_Doll_Files/`).
## Current state
- 86 loose PNG files at repo root
- Unclear asset strategy: `assets/`, `public/`, and loose files all coexist
- `revenue-sidecar/` is a Python DAFL service with Dockerfile (separate deployable)
- `project/` contains extract.js and test_worker.js (likely experimental)
- Well-organized `src/` structure exists (components, sim, rendering, utils, data)
## Acceptance criteria
- Every top-level folder has a clear purpose documented in README.
- No unexplained loose production assets at repo root.
- README explains app entrypoints, sim layer, rendering layer, persistence layer.
- Asset loading strategy is documented (public/ vs src/assets/ vs assets/).
- Build and import paths remain functional after reorganization.

issues.title: [Foundation][P0] Enforce strict domain typing across simulation, rendering, and persistence
body_lines: The custom instructions emphasize `src/types.ts` as the core domain model.
In a sim-heavy TypeScript game, type drift becomes the fastest way to create bugs.
## Scope
- Audit all state objects against `src/types.ts`.
- Remove `any`, unsafe casts, and duplicated inline types (319 instances of `any` found).
- Separate domain types from view-model/render-only types.
- Ensure Firestore DTOs are transformed explicitly into domain models.
- Add guards/serializers for save/load boundaries.
- Enable TypeScript strict mode in `tsconfig.json`.
## Current state
- 319 instances of `any` across src/*.ts and src/*.tsx
- Comprehensive `src/types.ts` exists (855 lines)
- tsconfig.json has `"noEmit": true` but no `"strict": true`
- Save system uses `SAVE_SCHEMA_VERSION 2` in saveManager.ts
## Acceptance criteria
- `"strict": true` enabled in tsconfig.json and `npm run lint` passes cleanly.
- Core game state is defined once in `src/types.ts` and reused everywhere.
- Persistence payloads are validated before entering sim state.
- No `any` types in critical paths (sim, rendering, persistence).
- Runtime type guards exist for save/load and Firebase boundaries.

issues.title: [Foundation][P1] Profile and optimize simulation loop and canvas rendering
body_lines: The custom instructions explicitly state that the sim loop and rendering must be highly optimized.
Single agents are good at focused performance passes with measurable outcomes.
## Scope
- Identify expensive per-frame allocations in simulation loop.
- Remove redundant sprite/image processing during active frames.
- Cache derived render state where possible.
- Separate fixed-timestep sim work from variable render work.
- Avoid React re-renders driven by high-frequency sim ticks unless necessary.
## Current state
- Clear separation exists: `/src/sim/` (simulation logic), `/src/rendering/` (render layer)
- Canvas rendering in DoL sprite components
- Graphics quality system implemented with LOD and caching
- No performance baselines documented
## Acceptance criteria
- Performance baselines documented (FPS, frame time, memory usage).
- Main loop avoids unnecessary object churn (profiling data included).
- Rendering path uses caching/memoization where appropriate.
- FPS / frame-time regression checks are documented.
- Separation between sim tick and render frame is clear and enforced.

issues.title: [Foundation][P1] Harden Firebase auth, Firestore schema, and save/load flows
body_lines: Persistence bugs in simulation games are brutal: corrupted saves, stale schemas, or invalid auth assumptions.
Firebase config and `firestore.rules` already exist, so this is a real subsystem needing hardening.
## Scope
- Define versioned save schema with migration paths.
- Add migration handling for older saves (currently SAVE_SCHEMA_VERSION 2).
- Review Firestore rules against actual read/write patterns.
- Ensure auth state is handled cleanly during boot and resume.
- Prevent partial or malformed save writes.
## Current state
- `firestore.rules` present (709 bytes)
- Firebase SDK in package.json dependencies
- `saveManager.ts` has `SAVE_SCHEMA_VERSION 2` and `migrateGameState()`
- No documented schema versioning strategy
## Acceptance criteria
- Save documents have a `version` field and migration logic.
- Load path validates and migrates data from older versions.
- Firestore rules match real client behavior (audit completed).
- Auth/loading edge cases are documented and tested.
- Save integrity checks prevent corrupted data from entering game state.

issues.title: [Foundation][P1] Set up GitHub Actions for typecheck, lint, test, and build
body_lines: One agent becomes much more effective once quality gates are automated.
This reduces regressions while the repo is still evolving.
## Scope
- Add CI workflow for install, lint, typecheck, test, and production build.
- Add PR checks to block bad merges.
- Optionally add preview/deploy workflow if hosting target is defined.
- Cache dependencies for faster runs.
## Current state
- 2 workflows exist: `dafl-sidecar-deploy.yml`, `sync-agentic-roadmap.yml`
- No CI workflow for main app
- Package.json scripts available: `npm run lint` (tsc --noEmit), `npm run test` (vitest run), `npm run build` (vite build)
- Test suite passes with 562 tests across 12 files
## Acceptance criteria
- Every PR runs CI automatically (lint + typecheck + test + build).
- Typecheck/build failures block bad merges (branch protection or documentation for setup).
- Workflow docs explain required secrets and deploy conditions.
- CI runs complete in under 5 minutes with dependency caching.
- Build artifacts are uploaded for manual testing if needed.
